author: @rogerbraintree9552
text: That could make a very good motor for operating a direct drive turntable for playing vinyl records.  One of the problems encountered with turntable building is finding a solution which does not involve the use of a motor that operates at frequencies around the 50-200 Hz band as this often sets up vibrations which are very difficult to quell which then goes as interference through the pickup cartridge.  Your motor right there could solve that problem at a significantly reduced cost to the other attempts that have been made in the past at making turntables run quietly.  The one you showed there could run way below audible frequencies for turning around a record at 33rpm.  The ideal motor would have a frequency of about 1Hz but this is very difficult to achieve.  However I can see your motor running with 6 coils at around 3Hz to achieve a rotation of 33 rpm which itself is one revolution every 2 seconds and this is way below audio frequency and so there are no prominent vibrations set up by the motor that would need to be mechanically filtered out.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
If I understand how this motor works, if you fit two drive coils, as you intend to do, and reverse the winding polarity on one of the coils, then a diode placed in series with each coil, would allow the positive and negative cycle of an AC supply to energise each coil in turn. One problem might be that of starting, as each coil would be energised at 50Hz, not allowing time for the rotor to overcome the inertia.
An alternative driver circuit, might be a T type flipflop, with a Schmitt trigger variable oscillator clock input. The Q and /Q output driving two power MOSFETs, to energise each of  the coils in turn. The assemble is very  reminiscent of a stepper motor, advancing one step on each clock cycle.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate cheers - like a soft start?

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  the cost of  components required  should be just  few pounds for a dc supply control corcuit, most likely  less than £5. 
As far as i can tell, a couple of power mosfers, a 74hc132 Schmitt, and a T type flip flop or a D type wired as a T type, a voltage regulator to run the logic and a few decoupling capacitors and a couple of resistors to pull down the MOSFET gates. 
Of course, you could just  buy the mosfets, and simply drive them from an Arduino Nano or similar microcontroller, then you could program the coil timing and thus the speed and direction of the motor.
A further refinement might be to add a hall effect sensor or opto gate, to sense the position of the rotor, much like the ubiquitous, low voltage, brushless, computer fan.
I suspect you will need back emf protection diodes on the coils, if the motor is switched like a unipolar stepper motor, they really are very similar structures.

author: @ColinWatters
text: A lot of people have built new motors like this but they never get around to measuring the efficiency. The output power can be calculated from the torque and rpm. The torque can be measured by allowing the case of the motor to rotate against a spring balance/scale.  The rpm can be measured a number of ways if you have an oscilloscope but optical rpm meters are available fir model aircraft use. Input power can be calculated from the voltage and current.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Will need convinced by seeing if increasing radius would increase torque.

I reason less torque with increasing r because flux length shortening force is akin to force between magnets half distance and force goes up 4 time etc. Increase distance and force drops off quicker than distance increase.
If  number of C poles isn't increased, nor they increase in size... Larger radius means more arc length per C pole.

Larger r will probably result in motor that wants to spin faster

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
@stevecummins324 That would depend on your type of controller. You could use the Cat as a starter and have it disconnected when the rotor rotates. A kick start if you will. Once you have rotation you can alter the speed by the timing of the coil energisation. similar to a bushless fan controller. 
It could get really interesting if you turn it into a generator by having the "rotor" spun by a motor and the energy taken from the coil being energised by the changing magnetic field produced by the magnets and atenuated by the rotor position! 

To be clear on this, I have not read upon such a device .... simply extrapolated effects from a different device!
The last time I had any real discussion on this subject was back in 1980? when I was doing a City and Guilds .... my memory is not good enough to be certain as I used to be ;0)

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: I think you do not have a rotating field with 2 C's. You need 3 C's for doing that. A stepper motor has 2 windings and does create a rotating field but in that case the rotor is a permanent magnet and current in reverse in a winding has a different effect. In your setup that is not the case. The rotor will rotate though but you need to give it a manual spin first. So will one C.

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
Very nice. If I were making a setup like that, I'd position the stator arms fairly far apart so the only flux path is through the rotor, not through an adjacent stator arm. Also, 3-phase will give you starting torque wherever you are. Maybe 3 stators and 4 poles on the rotor?

Also, an easy setup to control the current in the coils would be a slotted optical interruptor disk and optical rotary encoder (LM393 or similar) controlling a MOSFET per coil. Remember the flyback diodes to prevent voltage spikes on turnoff which would kill the MOSFETs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that mate - cheers

author: @AdricM
text: Two quick thoughts Robert,  what about your piles of shaded pole motors  use them as the coil and the armature gap, pre wound main coil,  heck you might even be able to un wrap the copper coils from the original rotor and re-use the original bushings.  add some neodymium magnets to the outside of the frame near the main coil see if you can get it to tick.    looking around my junk for a old spare shadedpole motor to now..    secondarily other than the permanent magnet being in the rotor.  this is starting to look surprisingly like a section of a stepper motor.  and in that case you might try sawing one apart if you have a spare one laying around.

author: @timothygorman7528

An aluminum rim with implanted magnetic steel strategically embedded along the rim, and two or three coils placed along the circular path of the rim?   This would provide a significant "torque arm."

author: @Kapalek84
text: The idea is very good but in order to create a flux path through the rotor segment it would be good to make it out of transformer steel laminations and align rotor segment laminations with stator laminations. Keeping the gap between rotor and stator to less then 1mm would also make the efficiency better. One last thing would be to make the stator C arms shorter to reduce flux losses. I hope this project will bring some great results! Best of luck!
